headline_metrics.nii: NII growth outlook lowered to 1–1.5% (full year)

nim_nii.summary: NIM fell 4 bps linked quarter to 2.98%, driven by higher deposit costs, lower loan spreads, and a larger balance sheet. Full-year NII guidance was cut to +1–1.5% from the prior +2–3%, driven by three headwinds: portfolio optimization reducing lower-return consumer loans, broad-based loan spread compression (now expected -5 to -10 bps year-over-year), and an unfavorable deposit mix shift toward higher-rate products; partially offset by higher medium/long-term rates.
nim_nii.quote: We now expect net interest income to increase approximately 1 to 1.5%. Our updated outlook reflects actions we have taken to improve profitability as well as certain market dynamics. First, we are continuing to optimize less strategic and lower return lending portfolios that offer limited relationship potential which has the effect of reducing NII and net interest margin but improves ROTCE.
nim_nii.speaker: Michael Maguire, Chief Financial Officer

credit_normalization.summary: Credit improved this quarter: net charge-offs fell 11 bps linked quarter to 50 bps annualized, and the allowance for loan losses dipped 2 bps to 1.51% of total loans. Full-year NCO guidance is held at 55 bps. A technical reclassification of indirect auto loans to non-accrual status lifted non-performing loan ratios by 1 bp but management emphasized this does not reflect deteriorating credit.
credit_normalization.quote: Net charge-offs declined 11 basis points linked quarter to 50 basis points, reflecting lower losses across most portfolios compared with the second quarter of 2025. Net charge-offs were relatively stable. Our provision for credit losses totaled $395 million, modestly below net charge-offs of $414 million, resulting in a 2 basis point linked quarter decline in allowance for loan losses to 1.51% of total loans.
credit_normalization.speaker: Michael Maguire, Chief Financial Officer

cre_reserves.summary: CRE reserves were modestly released this quarter — the overall ALLL declined 2 bps to 1.51% — driven in part by the resolution of several commercial and CRE problem credits and continued improvement in office and multifamily non-performing loans. Management signaled progress in both office and multifamily as drivers of the reserve reduction.
cre_reserves.quote: The modest reduction in our ALLL was primarily driven by the resolution of several commercial and commercial real estate problems. Credits during the quarter and continued improvement in sectors like office and multifamily non-performing loans held for investment increased 1 basis point linked quarter to 51 basis points of total loans as higher indirect auto problem loans were partially offset by improvement in the commercial portfolio.
cre_reserves.speaker: Michael Maguire, Chief Financial Officer

deposit_costs.summary: Deposit costs rose modestly: average interest-bearing deposit costs increased 1 bp linked quarter to 2.10% and total deposit costs rose 1 bp to 1.56%. The cumulative interest-bearing beta fell slightly to 45% from 46%. The bigger concern flagged by management is mix shift — DDA balances are expected to remix down from ~27% to ~25% of deposits by year-end as clients migrate into higher-rate products, which was cited as the single largest headwind to the revised NII outlook.
deposit_costs.quote: We continue to see healthy client deposit activity, however, deposit mix trends are being pressured by elevated rate-seeking behavior and migration into higher rate products. Average interest-bearing deposit costs increased by 1 basis point linked quarter to 2.10% and average total deposit cost increased 1 basis point to 1.56%.
deposit_costs.speaker: Michael Maguire, Chief Financial Officer

capital_return.summary: Truist repurchased $1.2B of common stock in Q2 (up from $1.1B in Q1 and $750M in Q2 2025) and maintained its full-year buyback target of $5B, returning over 100% of earnings to shareholders. The CET1 ratio rose 10 bps to 10.9% despite the elevated payout, reflecting strong capital generation and RWA optimization. Management targets 10% CET1 by end of 2027 and framed $5B as a prudent glide path, noting organic growth opportunities take priority over incremental buybacks.
capital_return.quote: During the second quarter we repurchased $1.2 billion of common stock compared with $1.1 billion in the prior quarter and $750 million in the second quarter of 2025. We continue to target approximately $5 billion of share buybacks in 2026.
capital_return.speaker: Michael Maguire, Chief Financial Officer

loan_growth.summary: All loan growth discussed is organic — no acquisitions were referenced. Average loans grew 0.7% linked quarter to $329B, driven by 1.3% growth in commercial loans (C&I up ~8% year-over-year) while consumer loans declined. Management is deliberately running off less profitable consumer portfolios (marine/RV exited, prime and non-prime auto production cut ~40%), reducing annual production by ~$7–8B. Full-year loan growth is expected in the low single-digit range, concentrated in C&I; consumer will be roughly flat to +1%.
loan_growth.quote: Average loans held for investment increased $2.1 billion or 0.7% linked quarter to $329 billion driven by 1.3% growth in average commercial loans partially offset by a decline in average consumer loans. End of period loans increased modestly linked quarter reflecting slight growth in both commercial and consumer.
loan_growth.speaker: Michael Maguire, Chief Financial Officer

macro_outlook.summary: Management did not cite explicit recession odds or a specific rate-path forecast. Their tone was cautiously constructive: consumer and commercial client activity is healthy, pipelines are solid, and deposit production is strong, but they flagged persistent rate-seeking deposit behavior, broad-based loan spread compression, and macro-driven uncertainty around AI's second-order economic effects as risks warranting discipline in portfolio concentration and capital allocation.
macro_outlook.quote: And as you point, I mean, we look at, you know, secondary and tertiary impacts in any particular market, particularly related to the investments in AI.
macro_outlook.speaker: Bill Rogers, Chairman and CEO

notable_qa.summary: Mike Mayo of Wells Fargo pressed Bill Rogers directly on the stock's decade-long underperformance, calling it 'dead money' while peer banks and the S&P nearly doubled, and asked what could be done better going forward. Rogers acknowledged the shared objective of building a high-performing company, pointed to technology investments, talent, and strategic clarity as the path forward, and framed the incoming CEO Mike Lyons as an accelerant — but declined to relitigate the past or offer specific new commitments.
notable_qa.quote: Hey, Bill, from the other comments, I do think you're a great human being, but as you know, I've been extremely disappointed about the results this decade where the stock has been kind of dead money when the stock banks are up almost half and the S&P is almost double. So I've been very frustrated over time.
notable_qa.speaker: Mike Mayo, Analyst at Wells Fargo Securities
